title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: Rising Tides: The Architecture of Adaptation
content.text: For much of human history, the proximity of the ocean was our greatest economic advantage. Today, for the 600 million people living in low-lying coastal areas, it has become their greatest threat. As sea levels rise, the traditional response of building higher walls and stronger dikes is beginning to look like a temporary fix. || (41) || [41] This radical shift in thinking involves moving away from 'fighting' the water toward 'living' with it [/41]. Instead of seeing the ocean as an invader, architects are exploring ways to integrate it into the urban fabric. The most ambitious of these proposals is the development of entire floating cities. || (42) || [42] Unlike traditional houseboats, these are designed to be self-sustaining urban ecosystems [/42]. They would be modular in design, allowing them to expand or contract based on the needs of the population. Constructed from reinforced concrete or lightweight composites, these platforms could support high-rise buildings, parks, and even urban farms. || (43) || [43] Such a project is already in the works off the coast of Busan, South Korea [/43]. Known as 'Oceanix City,' it aims to demonstrate that floating communities can be resilient to extreme weather events. The platforms will be anchored to the seabed but allowed to rise and fall with the tides. || (44) || [44] However, the challenges are not just structural; they are also psychological [/44]. Convincing people to leave the 'solid ground' for a floating platform requires a major cultural leap. Many worry about sea-sickness, a sense of isolation, or the long-term stability of their homes. || (45) || [45] To address these fears, developers are emphasizing the lifestyle benefits of aquatic living [/45]. Waterfront views, direct access to marine recreation, and the promise of a truly sustainable community are being used to market these projects to a skeptical public. But beyond the luxury market, there is a more urgent need for this technology. || (46) || [46] In regions like the Mekong Delta, millions are at risk of losing their livelihoods [/46]. For these communities, floating architecture isn't a futuristic luxury; it's a matter of survival. As the climate crisis intensifies, our ability to adapt to a wetter world will determine the fate of some of the planet's most vibrant cultures.

paragraphs.A: The financial cost is another significant barrier. Building on water is currently far more expensive than building on land, and finding insurers willing to cover a floating skyscraper is proving difficult. This may limit the early adoption to only the wealthiest nations.
paragraphs.B: [44] There is an ancient, hard-wired preference for the stability of land that is difficult to override [/44]. Even with modern engineering, the idea of one's home being at the mercy of the waves can be deeply unsettling for many.
paragraphs.C: [41] This has led to the emergence of what some call 'amphibious architecture' [/41]. Rather than trying to keep the water out, these designs allow it to flow into specific areas during floods, reducing the pressure on the rest of the city.
paragraphs.D: [46] These areas are seeing their traditional farmland inundated with saltwater, making it impossible to grow crops [/46]. Floating greenhouses and salt-tolerant aquaculture could provide the only path forward for these displaced farmers.
paragraphs.E: [42] The concept is no longer confined to the realms of science fiction [/42]. Major engineering firms are now producing detailed blueprints for large-scale floating neighborhoods that can withstand Category 5 hurricanes.
paragraphs.F: [43] Key to the success of such cities is their ability to produce their own food and energy [/43]. Solar panels would cover every available surface, and hydroponic systems beneath the platforms would allow for year-round vegetable production.
paragraphs.G: [45] In addition to these aesthetic draws, the environmental credentials of floating cities are impeccable [/45]. Because they aren't built on land, they have a zero-impact on local terrestrial ecosystems and can even help restore coral reefs by providing artificial structures for marine life.

questions.explanation: C introduce el término 'amphibious architecture', que encaja con el cambio de mentalidad de 'vivir con el agua'.
E dice que el concepto ya no es ciencia ficción, lo que da paso a la descripción técnica de los ecosistemas urbanos flotantes.
F habla de la producción de energía y comida, un detalle clave de la 'auto-suficiencia' mencionada en el texto principal.
B refuerza el punto psicológico sobre la 'preferencia por la tierra firme' mencionado justo antes del hueco 44.
G añade las 'credenciales ambientales' a los beneficios estéticos usados para convencer al público.
D conecta específicamente con el Delta del Mekong y el problema de los cultivos de los granjeros.
